items.question:
A 22-year-old woman is referred to you because of oligomenorrhea. She underwent menarche at age 12 years, but never developed a regular menstrual cycle and has had very infrequent periods. Her menses have become somewhat more irregular in the past year, but without a definitive pattern it is difficult for her to assess.

She takes no regular medications. She is sexually active but does not currently desire pregnancy, although she would like to have regular menstrual cycles.

She takes no regular medications.

On physical examination, height is 64.2 in (163 cm) and weight is 165 lb (75 kg) (BMI = 28.1 kg/m²). Pressure is 124/78 mm Hg. Findings on breast examination are normal, and she has normal secondary sexual characteristics. She has acne affecting her chin and mild facial hirsutism. There is no hyperpigmentation. From routine biochemical testing are normal.

Laboratory test results:

Androstenedione = 360 ng/dL (12.6 nmol/L)

Free testosterone = 3.9 ng/dL (0.14 nmol/L)

17α-Hydroxyprogesterone = 1080 ng/dL (32.7 nmol/L)

Prolactin = 18 ng/mL (0.78 nmol/L)

Estradiol = 75 pg/mL (275.3 pmol/L)

Which of the following is the most appropriate therapy to institute now?
options: Desogestrel (0.15 mg) and ethinyl estradiol (0.03 mg) combined oral contraceptive pill
Leuprolide acetate, 3.75 mg intramuscular injection monthly
items.explanation:
14 ANSWER: A) Desogestrel (0.15 mg) and ethinyl estradiol (0.03 mg) combined oral contraceptive pill. The combination of oligomenorrhea, elevated BMI, and clinical and biochemical evidence of hyperandrogenism make polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) a possible diagnosis. However, the high concentration of 17α-hydroxyprogesterone suggests that nonclassic (also known as late-onset) congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is, in fact, the etiology of this patient's symptoms. This could be confirmed by performing an ACTH-stimulation test, which would demonstrate a further rise in 17α-hydroxyprogesterone concentrations.

Late-onset CAH usually presents in late childhood, adolescence, or early adulthood and can be clinically indistinguishable from PCOS. Therefore, late-onset CAH should be excluded in all women who present with appt PCOS by measuring plasma 17α-hydroxyprogesterone (normal in PCOS and elevated in nonclassic CAH because reduced 21α-hydroxylase activity). However, one must be careful when interpreting 17α-hydroxyprogesterone level in premenopausal women. During the luteal phase, levels can approach 300 ng/dL (9.1 nmol/L), but the concentral should never exceed 1000 ng/dL (30.3 nmol/L) as was observed in this vignette.

Nonclassic CAH is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner and its prevalence varies according to ethm. Overall, the prevalence is estimated to be 1 in 1000 in white populations, but as high as 1 in 27 in women of Eastern European Jewish descent and 1 in 40 among persons of Hispanic ancestry.

In nonclassic CAH, mutations in the CYP21A2 gene lead to reduced, but not absent, 21α-hydroxylase activity. Disease-causing mutations are characterized as severe or mild, depending on the amount of residual enzyme activity. Persons with late-onset CAH typically have either 2 mild mutations or 1 mild mutation and 1 severe mutation, resulting in 20% to 60% of normal enzymatic activity. Therefore, adrenal cortisol production is maintained at the expense of increased ACTH drive to the adrenal, which leads to androgen excess and increased levels of precursor steroid such as 17α-hydroxyprogesterone. In contrast, individuals with classic CAH present birth with ambiguous genitalia (in girls) and adrenal insufficiency due to absent 21α-hydroxylase activity.

The young woman in this vignette has menstrual disturbance and other clinical features of androgen excess (facial acne and hirsutism). Given that she does not currently desire pregnancy, treatment should be aimed at addressing these symptoms. It is clear in the vignette that menstrual disturbance is the predominant problem, so the combined oral contraceptive pill (Answer A) is the most appropriate treatment. The oral contraceptive pill effectively suppresses ovarian androgens and thus may also address her acne and hirsutism.

Because women with nonclassic CAH have normal cortisol production, routine corticosteroid treatment to ensure adequate glucocorticoid levels is not required. However, exogenous glucocorticoid inhibition of ACTH also reduces adrenal androgen production (and 17α-hydroxyprogesterone) and would thereby alleviate her symptoms of hyperandrogenism and restore a normal menstrual cycle. However, glucocorticoid therapy is less appropriate than oral contraceptives for this patient. She does not currently desire fertility and glucocorticoid therapy would make her potentially fertile, thereby necessitating birth control if she were to become sexually active. A number of glucocorticoid regimens have been used for patients with CAH who desire pregnancy, including dexamethasone at bedtime (Answer D). However, the dosage used in these circumstances is usually 250 mcg. Thus, 2 mg nightly represents gross overreplacement and would result in iatrogenic Cushing syndrome.

Metformin (Answer C) can be used in the management of PCOS, but it is not recommended for the management of oligomenorrhea or hirsutism in CAH because the hyperandrogenism is not related to insulin resista
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 14: "Distinguish between the diagnoses of polycystic ovary syndrome and late-onset congenital adrenal hyperplasia and recommend appropriate treatment."

items.title: Why answer A is correct
items.text: Desogestrel (0.15 mg) and ethinyl estradiol (0.03 mg) combined oral contraceptive pill. However, the high concentration of 17α-hydroxyprogesterone suggests that nonclassic (also known as late-onset) congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is, in fact, the etiology of this patient's symptoms. This could be confirmed by performing an ACTH-stimulation test, which would demonstrate a further rise in 17α-hydroxyprogesterone concentrations.

items.question:
A 66-year-old woman is referred to you after her primary care physician documented an elevated TSH level at a routine visit. She has no specific concerns. Since retiring from her job a year ago, she has noticed an

improved quality of life with a regular exercise routine. She has managed to lose a little weight. She smokes 1 pack of cigarettes daily and has done so for 40 years. For personal reasons, she maintains a low-sodium diet, which is also low in iodine content.

On physical examination, weight is 182 lb (82.7 kg) and height is 64 in (162.6 cm) (BMI = 31.2 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 128/78 mm Hg. Her 20-g thyroid gland has no discrete nodules. Her physical examination findings are normal.

Laboratory test results:

Serum TSH = 6.5 mIU/L

TPO antibodies = 200 IU/mL (200 KIU/L)

Measurements 1 month later:

TSH = 7.0 mIU/L

Serum free  $ T_{4}=1.1 $ ng/dL (14.2 pmol/L)

She has no records of being screened for thyroid dysfunction in the past.

Which of the following factors confers the greatest future risk of her becoming overtly hypothyroid?
options: Obesity 38 A 19-year-old man is transitioning from the pediatric endocrinology clinic. In infancy, he was admitted to the hospital for new-onset hyperglycemia. He had presented with acute hyperglycemia (blood glucose concentration of 578 mg/dL [32.1 mmol/L]) associated with weight loss, polyuria, and polydipsia. He was initially treated with insulin and his regimen was subsequently transitioned to oral sulfonylurea treatment. He had done well with this treatment, reports good glycemic control, and has no diabetes-related complications. The patient's father has a history of diabetes mellitus that is well controlled with oral hypoglycemic therapy. Physical examination findings are normal. His hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ measurement is 6.3% (0.063).
items.explanation:
## 37 ANSWER: C) Presence of TPO antibodies

37 Subclinical hypothyroidism is diagnosed when a patient has an elevated serum TSH value accompanied by normal thyroid hormone levels. The natural history of subclinical hypothyroidism is particularly affected by 2 factors: the magnitude of the serum TSH elevation and the presence or absence of TPO antibodies.

Regarding TSH elevation, the study showed that 52% of patients documented to have baseline serum TSH values that were mildly elevated (5.0-9.9 mIU/L) had normal TSH values at the end of a 3-year period. The percentage of baseline TSH values in the ranges of 10.0 to 14.9 mIU/L and 15.0 to 19.9 mIU/L that normalized after 3 years in the same study were 13% and 5%, respectively. These observations have been confirmed in other studies. For example, in a study of patients older than 65 years, resolution of subclinical hypothyroidism occurred at the 2-year follow-up in 46% of those with TSH values between 4.5 and 6.9 mIU/L, in 10% of those with values between 7.0 and 9.9 mIU/L, and in 7% of those with values of 10.0 mIU/L or greater.

Nevertheless, there is an increased risk of developing overt hypothyroidism even with successively increasing baseline TSH values that fall within the normal range. This risk is affected by TPO antibody status. A study examined the risk of developing overt hypothyroidism during a longer follow-up period of 13 years according to initial TSH groupings of less than 2.5 mIU/L, 2.5 to 4.0 mIU/L, and greater than 4.0 mIU/L in patients with positive thyroid antibodies. Compared with an odds ratio of 1.4 in the group with TSH values less than 2.5 mIU/L, the odds ratio for hypothyroidism was 10 in the group with TSH values between 2.5 and 4.0 mIU/L and 108 for the group with TSH values greater than 4.0 mIU/L. The odds ratios were lower (1, 1.8, and 50, respectively) for the same TSH groups in patients without thyroid antibodies. Thus, some patients with mild TSH elevation, such as this patient, progress to overt hypothyroidism over prolonged periods and monitoring of thyroid function is indicated. A prospective study showed that the calculated risk ratios of overt hypothyroidism in the 3 TSH categories of less than 6.0 mIU/L, 6.0 to 12.0 mIU/L, and greater than 12.0 mIU/L were 1.2, 6.4, and 11.5, respectively, over 10 years of follow-up. These risk ratios increased to 5.3, 10.5, and 15.6 in patients with markedly elevated TPO antibody titers. Similarly, in one of the aforementioned studies, progression to overt hypothyroidism occurred with greater frequency in those with positive thyroid autoantibodies. Conversely, another study demonstrated that resolution of subclinical hypothyroidism is less likely in patients older than 65 years who have positive TPO antibodies than in those with negative antibodies (15% vs 48% of patients) (thus, Answer C is correct). The results of such studies have led to the recommendation to consider measurement of TPO antibodies as part of the evaluation of patients who have subclinical hypothyroidism.

However, in all these studies, patient age did not appear to predict progression to overt hypothyroidism (thus, Answer B is incorrect). Patient sex does not affect the likelihood of progression in some studies, although other studies do find a greater risk of progression in women. The reason for these discrepant findings with respect to sex may relate to the association between female sex and TPO antibodies, such that they are not independent factors. In the studies that have examined BMI, this factor did not affect the risk of developing overt hypothyroidism (thus, Answer E is incorrect). In some studies, the presence of a goiter increases the risk of future hypothyroidism. In one study of populations with different iodine intakes, a high-iodine intake is a further risk factor for development of overt hypothyroidism in patients with TPO antibodies (thus, Answer D is incorrect). Smoking has either been found to have no influence on p
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 37: "Explain the natural history of subclinical hypothyroidism and determine the factors most likely to affect its progression to overt hypothyroidism."

items.title: Why answer C is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 37: "Explain the natural history of subclinical hypothyroidism and determine the factors most likely to affect its progression to overt hypothyroidism."

items.title: How to apply this case clinically

items.question:
You are asked to see a 31-year-old man who recently moved to your area. Nonclassic congenital adrenal hyperplasia was diagnosed at age 8 years in the setting of precocious puberty. He never had salt wasting. Initial treatment consisted of hydrocortisone, 10 mg 3 times daily, and this regimen was continued for 13 years. His regimen was transitioned to dexamethasone, 1 mg at bedtime, at age 21 years. He began to gain weight at age 28 years, thought perhaps to be related to the dexamethasone, and therapy was stopped 3 years ago when his ACTH level was less than 5 pg/mL (1.1 pmol/L) and his 17-hydroxyprogesterone level was 82 ng/dL (2.5 nmol/L).

One year ago, he began to experience mood swings and hot flashes. A low testosterone level was documented along with a reduced sperm count on semen analysis. Dexamethasone was restarted at a dosage of 0.25 mg daily at bedtime. He has continued that regimen for the last year. He has not missed any doses of dexamethasone and takes no other medications.

On physical examination, he is a healthy-appearing man. Height is 65 in (165.1 cm), and weight is 156 lb (70.9 kg) (BMI = 26.0 kg/m $ ^{2} $). On examination of the skin, there is some moderate hyperpigmentation of the palmar creases. Otherwise, his examination findings, including those from genitourinary exam, are normal.

Laboratory test results:

Testosterone = 158 ng/dL (5.5 nmol/L)

ACTH = 93 pg/mL (20.5 pmol/L)

17-Hydroxyprogesterone=1732 ng/dL (54.5 nmol/L)

DHEA-S = 550  $ \mu $g/dL (14.9  $ \mu $mol/L)

Which of the following changes should be made to best manage his hypogonadism, altered fertility, and congenital adrenal hyperplasia?
options: Change dexamethasone to hydrocortisone, 10 mg at bedtime
Change dexamethasone to hydrocortisone, 5 mg at breakfast and at bedtime
Add topical testosterone gel, 5 g daily
Increase dexamethasone to 0.5 mg at bedtime
Increase dexamethasone to 0.25 mg at breakfast and at bedtime
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 49: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.title: Why answer A is correct
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Answer 49: "See ESAP 2015 discussion."

items.question:
A 22-year-old woman is referred to you for a second opinion regarding hyperaldosteronism and hypercortisolemia. She recently graduated from college and was found to have hypertension on a preemployment physical examination. She has a history of oligomenorrhea during her initial year of college. On contraceptives were started at that time and have been continued. She has not experienced headaches, palpitation or increased sweating. There is no family history of hypertension. She has no history of hypokalemia. She occasionally eats licorice. Studies performed to exclude secondary causes of hypertension demonstrate elevation of aldosterone and cortisol (see data below).

She does not smoke cigarettes, and she drinks 2 to 3 alcoholic beverages weekly. She is sexually active. Her medications include once-daily oral contraceptive pills containing drospirenone 3 mg/ethinyl estradiol 0.02 mg; multivitamin; omega-3 fatty acids, 1000 mg daily; and vitamin D, 1000 units daily.

On physical examination, she is a very healthy-appearing young woman. Blood pressure is 138/94 mm Hg without orthostatic change, and pulse rate is 78 beats/min. Height is 61.5 in (156.2 cm), and weight is 116 lb (52 kg) (BMI = 21.6 kg/m $ ^{2} $). She is noncushingoid, and findings on physical examination are normal.

Initial laboratory test results (sample drawn in the morning):

Comprehensive metabolic panel, normal TSH = 1.7 mIU/L

Aldosterone = 194 ng/dL (5382 pmol/L)

Plasma renin activity = 2.7 ng/mL per h

Cortisol = 34 µg/dL (938.0 nmol/L)

Basal morning ACTH = 24 pg/mL (5.3 pmol/L)

Urinary aldosterone = 84 µg/24 h (232.7 nmol/d)

Urinary sodium = 193 mmol/24 h (193 mmol/d)

Urinary free cortisol = 50 µg/24 h (138 nmol/d)

Overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test, cortisol = 2.6 µg/dL (71.7 nmol/L)

Late-night salivary cortisol = 0.05 µg/dL (1.5 nmol/L)

drenal CT performed before her visit with you showed normal adrenal glands.

which of the following should you recommend to manage her hypertension?
options: Initiate mifepristone, 300 mg daily Initiate ketoconazole, 200 mg twice daily . 63 A 78-year-old man is seen by his primary care physician for his annual check-up. Generally, he feels well and has no specific concerns, except for a gradual decrement in his energy level and strength. He continues to work part-time. He enjoys single-handed sailing and has noticed it is now harder to complete the tasks necessary to sail the boat by himself. His only chronic medical problems are hypertension, for which he takes hydrochlorothiazide, and osteoarthritis.

No thyroid hormone replacement A 29-year-old woman with polycystic ovary syndrome returns to your clinic for a routine follow-up appointment. She has a history of obesity, hirsutism, irregular menses, and infertility, but has no current plans for pregnancy. A previous evaluation for Cushing syndrome revealed no evidence of hypercortisolism. Prediabetes was diagnosed approximately 2 years ago on the basis of an elevated fasting glucose concentration and a hemoglobin $ A_{1c} $ value of 5.7% (0.057). At that time, the patient received comprehensive lifestyle education with an emphasis on weight loss through caloric restriction and regular physical activity. She reports that she exercises regularly and that she has been able to lose approximately 5% of her initial body weight, primarily by reducing her fat intake. The patient has taken metformin at a dosage of 1000 mg twice daily for the past year; she has tolerated the medication well and has had no difficulty with adherence.
items.explanation:
62 ANSWER: B) Discontinue the oral contraceptive. The assessment of elevated steroid concentrations must always account for the presence of factors (including medications) that may increase the levels of steroid-binding proteins, conditions that alter metabolic clearance of steroids, or medications that may directly affect steroid-receptor binding. This young woman is taking an oral contraceptive containing drospirenone and ethinyl estradiol. Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives are well known to cause marked increases in the major binding protein for cortisol (corticosteroid-binding globulin). Women taking oral contraceptives usually have basal total cortisol levels that exceed 20 μg/dL (552 nmol/L) and more than 50% have an abnormal result from an overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test. Although it is commonly thought that oral contraceptives do not affect urinary free cortisol excretion, a recent study showed that nearly one-third of women taking oral contraceptives had a mild elevation of urinary free cortisol. In contrast, late-night salivary cortisol (a reflection of biologically active free cortisol) is usually normal in these women. Consequently, this woman has no biochemical or clinical evidence of hypercortisolism, and the use of a glucocorticoid receptor antagonist such as mifepristone (Answer D) or a drug to inhibit adrenal steroidogenesis such as ketoconazole (Answer E) is not indicated. In contrast to cortisol, aldosterone has weak binding to corticosteroid-binding globulin, and estrogens do not significantly alter its concentration.

Drospirenone is a unique progestin that is actually an analogue of the antimineralocorticoid receptor antagonist spironolactone. The antimineralocorticoid activity of drospirenone made it popular because it tends to be associated with less fluid retention in young women; however, drospirenone has also been associated with an increased risk for thromboembolic disease, which has limited its use. Similar to spironolactone, drospirenone causes marked increases in plasma and urinary aldosterone because of the mineralocorticoid receptor blockade. Accordingly, plasma renin activity also increases. Therefore, the hyperaldosteronism in this patient is related to the antimineralocorticoid activity of drospirenone. Discontinuation of the oral contraceptive (Answer B) normalized her blood pressure. In a prospective cohort study in the United States of nearly 70,000 normotensive, female

nurses aged 25 to 42 years, the age-adjusted relative risk of hypertension when using oral contraceptives was 1.5 (95% confidence interval, 1.2-1.8) for current use and 1.1 (95% confidence interval, 0.9-1.2) for past use. After adjustment for age, body mass index, cigarette smoking, family history of hypertension, physical activity, alcohol intake, and ethnicity, current users of oral contraceptives had an increased risk for development of hypertension (relative risk, 1.8; 95% confidence interval, 1.5-2.3) compared with the risk for women who had never used oral contraceptives. The mechanism of how oral contraceptives increase blood pressure is unknown.

The vignette contains 2 other clues that this woman does not have true primary aldosteronism: (1) the nonsuppressed plasma renin activity and (2) the absence of hypokalemia with prodigious elevations of aldosterone. In patients with primary aldosteronism, the plasma renin activity is almost always less than 1 ng/mL per hour if the patient is not receiving any medications that alter the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. Even patients with primary aldosteronism who are taking an ACE inhibitor or an angiotensin-receptor antagonist rarely have a plasma renin activity greater than 1 ng/mL per hour. Hypokalemia would be inevitable in a patient with aldosterone concentrations of this magnitude in the absence of a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist. Although this woman has a markedly elevated aldosterone-to-plasma renin activity ratio (simply because th
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 62: "Identify the increases in aldosterone and cortisol due to steroid receptor blockade and changes in the steroid-binding protein concentration."

items.reference: ESAP 2015 Discussion 62: "Identify the increases in aldosterone and cortisol due to steroid receptor blockade and changes in the steroid-binding protein concentration."

items.question:
A 48-year-old woman is referred to you for low bone density, hypertension, and an incidentally discovered adrenal nodule. Because of her history of poorly controlled hypertension, a cardiologist performed a chest CT to exclude a coarctation of the aorta (negative), and a 1.4-cm left adrenal nodule was incidentally discovered (Hounsfield units = 2). In addition, an L2 vertebral compression fracture was noted. Her mean total hip T score as determined by DXA is -2.4 and her L1 through L4 T score is -1.9.

She does not smoke cigarettes, and she drinks 2 glasses of wine weekly. She has normal menstrual cycles, she has never been pregnant, and she has had a tubal ligation. There is no history of an eating disorder. Her family history is negative for hypertension and osteoporosis. Her estimated daily dietary calcium intake is 1100 mg. Current medications are lisinopril, amlodipine, and metoprolol.

On physical examination, blood pressure is 152/96 mm Hg and pulse rate is 64 beats/min. Weight is 129 lb (58.6 kg), and height is 64 in (162.6 cm) (BMI = 22.1 kg/m $ ^{2} $). She is noncushingoid, and her examination findings are normal.

25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 27 ng/mL (67.4 nmol/L)

PTH = 44 pg/mL (44 ng/L)

DHEA-S = 11  $ \mu $g/dL (0.30  $ \mu $mol/L)

#### Which of the following studies should you recommend next?
options: CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the left adrenal nodule
items.explanation:
73 ANSWER: C) Overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test

Adrenal-dependent (ACTH-independent) hypercortisolism is often documented in patients with incidentally 10% to 30% of discovered adrenal nodules. Depending on which biochemical criteria are used, approximately 10% of patients with adrenal nodules have evidence of dysregulated mild cortisol excess. Although many of these patients have absolutely no overt physical features of hypercortisolism, it is not unusual for them to have metabolic and clinical derangements that may be consequences of longstanding excessive cortisol secretion. These patients often have low bone mineral density with fractures, hypertension, and other features of the metabolic syndrome including truncal obesity and diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance.

All patients with adrenal nodules should undergo diagnostic evaluation to exclude a functioning lesion. Pheochromocytoma is diagnosed in 3% to 5% of patients with incidentally discovered adrenal nodules. However, the attenuation values of pheochromocytomas are always greater than 15 Hounsfield units; thus, the low attenuation value in this patient is consistent with an adrenal adenoma and excludes an adrenomedullary tumor. Therefore, measurement of plasma free metanephrines (Answer B) is not needed. Aldosterone-secreting adenomas rarely present as incidental adrenal nodules. The elevated plasma renin activity in this patient excludes this diagnosis and a saline suppression test for aldosterone (Answer D) is not indicated. CT-guided percutaneous adrenal biopsy (Answer E) is never needed in patients with low-attenuation adrenal nodules.

The most reliable and sensitive way to exclude adrenal-dependent hypercortisolism is with the overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test (Answer C). Although there is no consensus as to what constitutes a normal postdexamethasone cortisol concentration, many guidelines have suggested that cortisol should suppress to less than 1.8 μg/dL (<49.7 nmol/L). Nonetheless, some believe that a higher cortisol cutoff should be used in patients with incidentally discovered adrenal nodules. Interestingly, studies have shown that the visceral fat-to-total fat ratio is significantly increased in patients with adrenal nodules whose cortisol level after dexamethasone administration exceeds 1.8 μg/dL (49.7 nmol/L). This particular cutoff appears to represent a threshold for the increase in visceral fat; cortisol levels greater than 3 μg/dL (>82.8 nmol/L) or greater than 5 μg/dL (>137.9 nmol/L) are not associated with any further increases in the visceral fat in these patients.

Clearly, other diagnostic criteria in addition to the overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test must be used to characterize the presence or absence of hypercortisolism. Typically these patients have a low basal ACTH concentration (<10 pg/mL [<2.2 pmol/L]) and an attenuated ACTH response to corticotropin-releasing hormone. This suppressed ACTH often lowers adrenal androgen production and most such patients have a low DHEA-S level (as seen in this woman). Urinary free cortisol levels (measured by tandem mass spectrometry) are often normal in these patients and provide very poor sensitivity. Late-night salivary cortisol levels may also be normal, but they are frequently intermittently elevated or in the upper reference range. Therefore, all of these diagnostic criteria (overnight 1-mg dexamethasone suppression test, basal morning plasma ACTH, late-night salivary cortisol, urinary cortisol, and DHEA-S) must be considered when establishing the presence or absence of adrenal-dependent hypercortisolism.

DHEA-S) must be considered when establishing the vertebral compression fracture seen in this woman may be related to her mild ACTH-independent hypercortisolism. One of the most peculiar features of glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis is the large individual variability in its clinical presentation and severity. Low bone density with fractures (particularly ve

items.question:
A 19-year-old man is referred to you 1 week after pituitary surgery for ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome. He had presented 16 months ago with diffuse acne and was treated with minocycline. He developed hypertension and was prescribed lisinopril. He developed facial rounding and wide violaceous striae on his abdomen. He had marked muscle weakness, hypokalemia (potassium = 2.6 mEq/L [2.6 mmol/L]), and peripheral edema. An endocrinologist established the diagnosis of ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome. Pituitary
MRI showed a 6-mm, left-sided, hypoenhancing lesion (arrow). A neurosurgeon performed a left hemihypophysectomy. The histopathology showed normal pituitary tissue with Crooke hyaline changes. The patient was treated with dexamethasone, 1 mg every 12 hours, after the operation. On the third postoperative day, his serum cortisol concentration was 81 $ \mu $g/dL (2234.6 nmol/L) and his plasma ACTH concentration was 551 pg/mL (121.2 pmol/L). The dexamethasone was discontinued. His mother and father bring him to your office 1 week after surgery in a wheelchair. On physical examination, he is a very cushingoid-appearing young man with facial rounding and increased supraclavicular fullness. Blood pressure is 172/112 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 86 beats/min. Height is 68 in (172.7 cm), and weight is 281 lb (127.7 kg) (BMI = 42.7 kg/m²). He has diffuse acne on his face and trunk. There is increased pigmentation in his palmar creases. He has wide violaceous striae on his abdomen, chest, arms, shoulders, and thighs. He has severe proximal muscle weakness and 3+ pitting edema in his legs and his arms. You confirm hypokalemia and hyperglycemia on laboratory investigation. Preoperative biochemistry: Late-night salivary cortisol = 1.7 μg/dL (48 nmol/L) Basal plasma ACTH = 398 pg/mL (87.6 pmol/L) Plasma renin activity = <0.1 ng/mL per h Aldosterone = 1.0 ng/dL (27.7 pmol/L) Postoperative biochemistry: Late-night salivary cortisol = 1.96 μg/dL/dL (54 nmol/L) Basal plasma ACTH = 667 pg/mL (146.7 pmol/L) Which of the following should be your next step?
options: Inferior petrosal sinus sampling for ACTH
No further testing; wait for possible late remission of Cushing disease
CT of the chest and abdomen
High-dose dexamethasone suppression testing with measurement of serum cortisol
items.explanation:
104 ANSWER: C) CT of the chest and abdomen

The diagnosis and differential diagnosis of Cushing syndrome is a challenging problem in clinical medicine. The clinical presentations are protean and the typical classic phenotype overlaps with the ubiquitous metabolic syndrome. The clinical presentation of Cushing syndrome in young postpubertal patients is often heralded by acne and the development of stria. Surprisingly, children and teenagers with Cushing syndrome are very good students despite the same degree of cortisol-induced cerebral atrophy that is seen in adults. The development of facial rounding, progressive hypertension with edema, and proximal myopathy were enough to inaugurate a biochemical evaluation.

The biochemical confirmation of ACTH-dependent hypercortisolism was easy in this patient. He had marked elevations of basal cortisol secretion accompanying his clinically obvious Cushing syndrome. The elevation in plasma ACTH confirmed an ACTH-secreting neoplasm, resulting in the pituitary imaging study. Pituitary MRI showed a hypoenhancing lesion (really seen on only 1 slice image) in the left pituitary. Pituitary imaging abnormalities such as this are common in the normal population and must be interpreted with great caution in patients with ACTH-dependent hypercortisolism. Moreover, the dramatic increases in plasma ACTH and the prodigious hypercortisolism should alert clinicians to the very likely possibility of a nonpituitary (ectopic) source of ACTH hypersecretion. Although patients with atypical corticotroph macroadenomas may generate ACTH levels of the magnitude seen here, patients with ACTH-secreting pituitary microadenomas almost never have an ACTH concentration greater than 200 pg/mL (44 pmol/L). Therefore, a young man with an ACTH level greater than 500 pg/mL (>110 pmol/L) and hypokalemia after a failed (and unnecessary) pituitary surgery must have a nonpituitary ACTH-secreting tumor accounting for his severe hypercortisolism. Although inferior petrosal sinus sampling for ACTH (Answer A) is the often the only way to confirm the presence or absence of an ACTH-secreting pituitary tumor, noninvasive body imaging should definitely be considered before this procedure in this young man. The finding of Crooke hyaline changes reflects histologic changes in pituitary corticotroph cells resulting from any condition associated with a state of hypercortisolism (including iatrogenic Cushing syndrome). It is not specific for pituitary ACTH-dependent hypercortisolism.

The most likely location of an ectopic ACTH-secreting tumor in a young man is in the thorax. Most of these ectopic ACTH-secreting tumors are neuroendocrine tumors in the chest (and sometimes in the abdomen), so most authorities believe that a CT of the chest and abdomen (Answer C) is the most sensitive initial screening test. Small bronchial carcinoids can be confused with pulmonary vessels on CT or MRI imaging. Small thymic masses are seen on MRI in about one-third of patients with Cushing disease and these do not always represent neuroendocrine tumors. This finding usually represents residual normal thymic tissue, particularly in patients younger than 40 years. In fact, thymic hyperplasia may actually occur in young patients after correction of their hypercortisolism. This thymic enlargement can sometimes be mistaken for an occult ectopic source of ACTH hypersecretion after medical or surgical correction of the Cushing syndrome.

Other imaging modalities, such as 18-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography (PET), have not been shown to be better than CT or MRI. The sensitivity of octreotide acetate scintigraphy for detecting occult tumors that secrete ACTH ranges from 30% to 53%. This may be due to their small size or also to inadequate expression of somatostatin receptors that are down-regulated by the ambient hypercortisolism. Some patients with pituitary

ACTH-secreting tumors may have a late remission of their hypercortisolism and conservative m
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 104: "Diagnose ectopic ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome in patients with prodigious hypercortisolism and hypokalemia."

items.question:
A 53-year-old woman is referred to you because of resistant hypertension. Despite adherence to her treatment regimen (amlodipine, 10 mg daily; hydrochlorothiazide, 25 mg daily; and ramipril, 10 mg daily), she has not had adequate control of her blood pressure over the past 18 months. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable.
On physical examination, blood pressure is 162/98 mm Hg. There are no signs of hypercortisolism. Height is 64.5 in (163.8 cm), and weight is 174 lb (79.1 kg) (BMI = 29.4 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Biochemical testing confirms primary aldosteronism. Adrenal CT is subsequently performed, which shows a 1-cm left adrenal adenoma (see image, arrow) with a density of 2 Hounsfield units and greater than 50% washout 10 minutes after intravenous contrast. The patient is very interested in surgical treatment, if appropriate, because she wishes to minimize her drug therapy. You therefore arrange for adrenal venous sampling (without cosyntropin stimulation), and the results are summarized in the table.
MeasurementRight Adrenal VeinLeft Adrenal VeinInferior Vena Cava
Aldosterone18 ng/dL\n(499 pmol/L)61 ng/dL\n(1692 pmol/L)19 ng/dL\n(527 pmol/L)
Cortisol10 $ \mu g/dL $\n(276 nmol/L)43.2 $ \mu g/dL $\n(1192 nmol/L)11 $ \mu g/dL $\n(303 nmol/L)
Aldosterone-to-cortisol ratio1.814.31.72
On the basis of these results and the information you have thus far, which of the following is the best course of action?
options: A second adrenal venous sampling procedure
items.explanation:
## 118 ANSWER: D) A second adrenal venous sampling procedure

Primary aldosteronism is very common among persons with drug-resistant hypertension and this is one of the criteria that should prompt screening for this condition. Once primary aldosteronism has been confirmed, further tests are needed to establish whether aldosterone excess is due to bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (idiopathic primary aldosteronism) or an aldosterone-producing adenoma. This distinction is very important because bilateral adrenal hyperplasia should be treated medically with mineralocorticoid antagonists, whereas unilateral aldosterone-producing adenomas can be cured (or at least improved) with unilateral adrenalectomy.

The adrenal CT in this vignette demonstrates a small left adrenal adenoma, which may be an aldosterone-producing adenoma potentially amenable to surgical treatment. However, given the incidence of adrenal incidentaloma in persons older than 40 years, as well as the fact that CT is not sufficiently sensitive to detect adrenal micronodules that may be present in bilateral adrenal hyperplasia, it is recommended that patients older than 40 years undergo further testing with adrenal venous sampling (AVS) to confirm unilateral aldosterone excess before surgery is recommended. This can be done with or without cosyntropin stimulation, as there is conflicting evidence as to whether cosyntropin improves the diagnostic accuracy of AVS. However, it is important to know whether cosyntropin has been used to aid interpretation of AVS as detailed in the following text.

The key to answering the vignette's question is the accurate interpretation of the AVS results; some guidance is outlined:

## 1. Ensure both adrenal veins have been cannulated (Selectivity Index)

Measurement of the ratio of cortisol in the adrenal vein to cortisol in the inferior vena cava calculates the selectivity index. If adrenal vein cannulation is successful, cortisol measurements should be significantly higher here than in the inferior vena cava. A selectivity index cutoff greater than 2 (with cosyntropin) or 3 (without cosyntropin) suggests successful cannulation. Some centers also advocate the measurement of plasma epinephrine in the adrenal veins and peripheral circulation to confirm successful cannulation.

## 2. Determine whether there is unilateral aldosterone excess (Lateralization Index)

To do this, the ratio of aldosterone to cortisol must first be calculated within each adrenal vein. These so-called cortisol-corrected ratios of aldosterone are performed to overcome the potential dilution of aldosterone levels within the adrenal veins by blood flow from the accessory hepatic and inferior phrenic veins. Subsequently, the aldosterone to cortisol ratios between the affected and unaffected side should be calculated (the lateralization index). Current guidelines suggest that a lateralization index greater than 2 without cosyntropin and greater than 4 with cosyntropin is suggestive of lateralized aldosterone excess.

In this vignette, the selectivity index on the right side is 0.9, which suggests that the right adrenal vein was not cannulated. This is a common problem given the small size and awkward positioning of the right adrenal vein. Therefore, although it would appear that the left adrenal is producing excess aldosterone, this is impossible to confirm using these data.

The best course of action under these circumstances would be to recommend another AVS procedure (Answer D) or to simply commence medical therapy. However, the vignette emphasizes that the patient would prefer a surgical cure if possible. Thus, in this situation, repeated AVS is the correct step.

Aldosterone-producing adenomas and benign adrenal adenomas are not fluorodeoxyglucose-PET avid, so a PET-CT scan (Answer A) would not help in securing the correct diagnosis. There has, however, been some evidence that specialized PET-CT scanning using 11C-metomidate tracer may help identify aldosterone-produ
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Question 118: "Interpret the results of adrenal venous sampling in the management of primary aldosteronism."
